metadata.description: Seed context for an Actor-Critic agentic system operating in the global trade domain. Covers trade finance, customs, logistics, compliance, and key regulatory frameworks.

structural_role.actor_persona: You are a global trade specialist AI with deep expertise in international trade finance, customs compliance, supply chain logistics, and cross-border regulatory frameworks. You provide accurate, actionable, and regulation-aware responses. Always cite the relevant regulation, Incoterm, or trade agreement when making claims about compliance or legal obligations.
structural_role.critic_persona: You are a senior trade compliance reviewer. Evaluate Actor responses for regulatory accuracy, Incoterm correctness, jurisdiction specificity, and actionability. Flag any response that generalises across jurisdictions without noting regional differences. Propose context updates only when a fact is verifiable and adds durable value.
domain_scope: Trade finance instruments: LC, SBLC, bank guarantees, documentary collections, open account
Customs and import/export compliance: HS codes, tariff classification, duty calculation
Trade agreements and preferential tariffs: FTAs, GSP, RCEP, USMCA, EU trade deals
Logistics and freight: FCL, LCL, air freight, multimodal, bonded warehousing
Sanctions and export controls: OFAC, BIS EAR, EU dual-use regulation
Trade documentation: bill of lading, certificate of origin, packing list, commercial invoice
Risk management: country risk, counterparty risk, currency risk, political risk

parties.name: Exporter / Seller
parties.role: The party selling and shipping goods across borders. Responsible for export declaration, export licence if applicable, and complying with the Incoterm obligations.
key_obligations: Prepare commercial invoice, packing list, certificate of origin
Comply with sanctions screening on buyer and destination country

parties.name: Importer / Buyer
parties.role: The party receiving and paying for goods. Responsible for import customs clearance, duty payment, and import permit where applicable.
key_obligations: Pay applicable duties, VAT/GST, and levies
Obtain import licence for controlled goods

parties.name: Freight Forwarder
parties.role: Intermediary that arranges cargo bookings, documentation, and customs brokerage on behalf of exporter or importer.
key_obligations: Issue House Bill of Lading or Air Waybill

parties.name: Issuing Bank
parties.role: In a Letter of Credit transaction, the bank that opens the LC on behalf of the buyer and undertakes to pay the seller upon compliant document presentation.

parties.name: Advising / Confirming Bank
parties.role: Bank in the seller's country that advises or adds confirmation to the LC, providing additional payment security to the exporter.

parties.name: Customs Authority
parties.role: Government body responsible for enforcing import/export laws, collecting duties and taxes, and preventing prohibited goods from crossing the border.

parties.name: Carrier
parties.role: The party physically transporting goods. Issues the Master Bill of Lading or Master Air Waybill.
parties.key_document: Bill of Lading (B/L) — functions as receipt, contract of carriage, and document of title

instruments.name: Letter of Credit (LC)
instruments.key_principle: Payment is triggered by compliant document presentation, not delivery of goods. Banks deal in documents, not goods.
instruments.critical_rule: Any discrepancy in documents (even minor typographical errors) entitles the issuing bank to refuse payment under UCP 600 Article 16.

instruments.name: Documentary Collection
instruments.risk_level: Lower bank risk than LC — banks do not guarantee payment, only handle document exchange.

instruments.name: Open Account
instruments.description: Goods shipped and documents sent directly to buyer. Payment made after delivery on agreed terms (e.g. net 30, net 60).
instruments.risk: Highest risk for exporter — no bank payment undertaking.
instruments.mitigation: Trade credit insurance, export factoring, supply chain finance

incoterms_2020.critical_note: Incoterms define risk transfer and cost allocation between buyer and seller. They do NOT determine title transfer or payment terms.

rules.seller_obligation: Minimum — make goods available at named place
rules.buyer_obligation: Maximum — bears all costs and risks from seller's door

rules.risk_transfer: When goods handed to carrier at named place
rules.suitable_for: Any mode including containerised sea freight
rules.note: 2020 update: allows B/L to be issued with 'on board' notation for LC purposes even under FCA

rules.risk_transfer: When goods handed to first carrier

rules.term: CIP — Carriage and Insurance Paid To
rules.risk_transfer: When goods handed to first carrier
rules.cost_to_seller: Freight + insurance (Institute Cargo Clauses A — all risks)
rules.note: 2020 update: increased insurance minimum to Clause A for CIP (Clause C remains for CIF)

rules.risk_transfer: At named destination, ready for unloading
rules.cost_to_seller: All costs to destination except import duties

rules.risk_transfer: Once goods unloaded at named destination
rules.note: New in 2020, replaces DAT. Only term where seller bears risk during unloading.

rules.risk_transfer: At named destination after import clearance
rules.cost_to_seller: Maximum — includes import duties and taxes
rules.note: Seller must be able to import in buyer's country. Problematic if seller is not VAT registered there.

rules.risk_transfer: When goods placed alongside vessel at port of shipment
rules.suitable_for: Bulk / break-bulk sea freight only (NOT containers)

rules.risk_transfer: When goods on board vessel at port of shipment
rules.common_misuse: Widely misused for containerised shipments — FCA should be used instead for containers

rules.risk_transfer: When goods on board vessel at port of shipment
When goods on board vessel at port of shipment
rules.cost_to_seller: Freight + insurance minimum (Institute Cargo Clauses C)
rules.lc_usage: Most commonly specified in Letters of Credit for commodity trade

hs_codes.description: Harmonized System (HS) codes are a globally standardised 6-digit numerical taxonomy for classifying traded goods, maintained by the World Customs Organization (WCO). Countries extend to 8–10 digits for national tariff purposes.
key_facts: 7+ digits = National tariff line — varies by country
Mis-classification is the most common cause of duty underpayment penalties
Classification is determined by the good's essential character at time of import, not its end use

common_chapters.description: Nuclear reactors, boilers, machinery and mechanical appliances

trade_agreements.name: USMCA
trade_agreements.key_benefit: Preferential (zero or reduced) tariffs between US, Mexico, Canada for qualifying originating goods
trade_agreements.origin_rule: Rules of Origin must be met — Regional Value Content (RVC) or Tariff Classification Change (TCC) tests
trade_agreements.critical_document: USMCA Certificate of Origin (self-certified by exporter)

trade_agreements.name: RCEP
trade_agreements.key_benefit: Largest free trade area by GDP — progressive tariff reduction over 20 years
trade_agreements.origin_rule: Regional Value Content >= 40% or Change in Tariff Classification

trade_agreements.name: EU Trade Agreements
trade_agreements.note: EU negotiates as a single customs union. Key FTAs include EU-South Korea, EU-Japan (EPA), EU-Vietnam, EU-Singapore, EU-Canada (CETA).
trade_agreements.preferential_scheme: EU Generalised Scheme of Preferences (GSP) — reduced duties for developing country exports

trade_agreements.name: AfCFTA
trade_agreements.status: Implementation ongoing — tariff schedules still being negotiated by many members

business_rules: Always verify that the HS code used for duty calculation matches the national tariff schedule of the import country — 6-digit HS subheadings are harmonised globally but duty rates are set nationally.
A Letter of Credit is irrevocable once issued under UCP 600 — the issuing bank cannot cancel it without consent of all parties.
Sanctions screening must be performed on all parties in a transaction (buyer, seller, carrier, banks, and ultimate consignee) before any trade finance instrument is issued or goods are shipped.
Certificate of Origin must be issued before or at the time of export — backdated certificates are not accepted by most customs authorities.
Incoterms 2020 FOB and CIF are only appropriate for non-containerised (bulk/break-bulk) cargo. For FCL/LCL containerised shipments, FCA and CIP are the correct equivalents.
Transfer of risk under Incoterms is independent of payment terms. Risk can transfer at origin even if payment is on net-60 open account.
Advance Rulings from customs authorities on HS classification and origin are legally binding on that authority for a defined period — always seek one for high-volume or ambiguous goods.
Dual-use goods require an export licence even if destined for civilian use if the end-user is flagged or the destination country is under trade restrictions.
Demurrage (container detention at port) accrues from the first day after free time expires — typically 3–7 days depending on port and carrier contract.
Under UCP 600, banks have a maximum of 5 banking days to examine documents and determine compliance after presentation.

terminology_glossary.definition: Bill of Lading — ocean carrier's receipt for goods, contract of carriage, and document of title. Can be negotiable (order B/L) or non-negotiable (straight/sea waybill).
Air Waybill — non-negotiable transport document for air freight. Consignee can collect goods without presenting original AWB.
Certificate of Origin — document certifying the country of manufacture of goods. Required to claim preferential tariff rates under FTAs.
Letter of Credit — bank instrument where issuing bank undertakes to pay exporter upon compliant document presentation.
Full Container Load — shipper has exclusive use of a 20ft or 40ft container.
Less than Container Load — cargo consolidated with other shippers' goods in a shared container.
Delivered Duty Paid — Incoterm where seller bears maximum responsibility including import duties.
Export Administration Regulations — US regulations governing export of dual-use items, administered by BIS.
Office of Foreign Assets Control — US Treasury agency that administers economic sanctions.
Generalised System of Preferences — unilateral tariff preference programs offered by developed countries to developing nations.
Free Trade Agreement — bilateral or multilateral agreement reducing tariffs and trade barriers between signatories.
Most Favoured Nation — WTO principle requiring a country to offer the same tariff rate to all WTO members unless a preferential agreement exists.
Regional Value Content — percentage of a product's value that must originate within the FTA zone to qualify for preferential tariff.
Tariff Classification Change — rules of origin test requiring the HS code of the finished product to differ from that of non-originating inputs.
International customs document allowing temporary duty-free import of goods (e.g. trade show samples, professional equipment) into participating countries.
Customs procedure allowing importation of goods for processing/manufacturing and re-export with full or partial duty relief.
Transport International Routier — customs transit system allowing road cargo to cross multiple countries under a single customs guarantee.

user_preferences.output_format: Structured response with: (1) direct answer, (2) applicable regulation or Incoterm cited, (3) jurisdiction-specific caveats where relevant, (4) recommended next action.
user_preferences.tone: Professional, precise, jurisdiction-aware. Assume a senior trade finance or supply chain professional audience.

relevance.description: Does the response directly address the task?

regulatory_accuracy.description: Are regulations, Incoterms, and trade rules cited correctly?

jurisdiction_specificity.description: Are jurisdiction-specific differences noted where they exist?

actionability.description: Can a trade professional act on this response without further research?

completeness.description: Are all materially relevant aspects of the task addressed?

retrieved_context.note: This section is populated dynamically each cycle and never persisted.
